criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 and 3.2 to understand masked diffusion models, the absorbing forward process, and ancestral sampling inference.
The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the definitions of confidence score and KL score as the two key metrics used by KLASS.
The agent has read Section 4.2 and Appendix B to understand the full KLASS algorithm, including stable-token selection, unmasking rule, and the fallback mechanism.

The agent has read Section 6.1 and Appendix D.1.1 to understand the reasoning task experimental setup: LLaDA 8B Instruct and Dream 7B Instruct, generation length 256, LLaDA block size 64, temperature 0 for LLaDA and 0.2 for Dream, and max inference steps 256.
The agent has read Table 1 to understand the performance results of KLASS and all baselines on GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, and MBPP.

The agent has read Section 6.2 and Appendix D.2.1 to understand the text generation experimental setup with MDLM, including 1000 sequences of length 1024, 512-step schedule, top-p=0.9, KL threshold 1e-4, and confidence threshold 0.57.

The agent has read Section 6.3 and Appendix D.3.1 to understand the image generation setup with MMaDA, including 10,000 class-conditional images, 16/32 decoding steps, KL threshold 0.3, confidence threshold 0.1, and history length 1.

The agent has read Section 6.4 and Appendix D.4.1 to understand the molecular generation setup with QM9, including classifier-free guidance (CFG), DIT architecture (12 blocks, hidden dim 768), 25000 gradient steps, KL threshold 0.001, and confidence threshold 0.98.
The plan includes computing a per-token confidence score as the maximum predicted probability over the vocabulary at each diffusion timestep.
The plan includes computing a per-token KL score as the KL divergence between the current and previous timestep's predicted probability distribution.

The plan includes maintaining a rolling history buffer of size n per token to track the last n KL divergence values, and checking that all n values are below the KL threshold for stable-token selection.
The plan includes the unmasking rule: unmask all stable tokens (those passing both KL and confidence criteria), and fall back to unmasking the top-u tokens by confidence when no stable tokens exist.
The plan includes the reasoning task experiments on GSM8K, MATH500, HumanEval, and MBPP using LLaDA 8B Instruct and Dream 7B Instruct.
The plan includes all five baseline samplers for reasoning tasks: Top-1, Random, Top-2, confidence-threshold (>0.9), and KL-threshold (<0.001 with history n=2).
The plan includes the text generation experiment on MDLM with OpenWebText, with MAUVE, generative perplexity (LLaMA2 7B, LLaMA3 8B, GPT-2), and Shannon entropy as evaluation metrics.
The plan includes molecular generation training with CFG on the QM9 dataset for QED and ring count conditions, using a DIT model and separate inference with KLASS.
The confidence score is implemented as the maximum value of the softmax probability distribution over the vocabulary for each token position at each timestep, as defined in Equation 5.
The KL score is implemented as the token-level KL divergence D_KL(p_t^i || p_{t+1}^i) between the current and previous timestep's predicted distributions, as defined in Equation 6.
A rolling KL history buffer of length n is implemented per token, storing and updating the last n per-token KL divergence values across consecutive decoding steps.

The stable-token selection criterion is implemented: a token is selected as stable if all n values in its KL history buffer are below the KL threshold epsilon_KL AND its confidence score exceeds the threshold tau AND the token is still masked.
The unmasking rule is implemented: if any stable tokens exist, all of them are unmasked in parallel; otherwise, the fallback mechanism unmasks the top-u masked tokens by confidence score.

The KLASS algorithm initializes with a fully masked sequence and a zeroed previous probability distribution and KL buffer, then iterates for T diffusion steps, returning the generated sequence after all tokens are unmasked.
The code loads and uses LLaDA 8B Instruct for reasoning tasks, setting generation length to 256, block size to 64 (semi-autoregressive), and temperature to 0.
The code loads and uses Dream 7B Instruct for reasoning tasks, setting generation length to 256 and temperature to 0.2.

The code implements per-task, per-model hyperparameter configurations for KLASS on reasoning benchmarks: LLaDA uses (conf=0.6, KL=0.010) on MATH, (conf=0.6, KL=0.015) on GSM8K, (conf=0.9, KL=0.010) on HumanEval, (conf=0.7, KL=0.010) on MBPP.

The code implements per-task, per-model hyperparameter configurations for KLASS on reasoning benchmarks: Dream uses (conf=0.9, KL=0.005) on MATH, (conf=0.9, KL=0.001) on GSM8K, (conf=0.8, KL=0.001) on HumanEval, (conf=0.9, KL=0.001) on MBPP.

The code implements all five baseline samplers for reasoning tasks: Top-1 (highest confidence per step), Random, Top-2 (two highest confidence per step), confidence-threshold (unmask all tokens with probability >0.9), and KL-threshold (unmask all tokens with KL <0.001, history n=2).
The code evaluates reasoning benchmarks using pass@1 accuracy and reports the number of sampling steps for each method and dataset.

The code implements KLASS for text generation with MDLM, using 1000 sequences of length 1024, a fixed 512-step schedule, nucleus top-p filtering at p=0.9, history length n=2, KL threshold 1e-4, and confidence threshold 0.57.

The text generation evaluation code computes generative perplexity under three oracle models (LLaMA2 7B, LLaMA3 8B, GPT-2), Shannon entropy, and MAUVE by comparing 1000 generated samples against 1000 held-out OpenWebText segments.

The code implements KLASS for image generation with MMaDA, generating 10,000 class-conditional images with uniform ImageNet labels under 16-step and 32-step schedules, with history n=1, KL threshold 0.3, and confidence threshold 0.1.
The image generation evaluation code computes FID (against 50k ImageNet validation set using Inception-v3) and IS for both confidence-based and KLASS samplers at 16-step and 32-step budgets.

The code trains independent masked diffusion transformer (DIT) models for molecular generation conditioned on QED and ring count using CFG with dropout probability 0.1, 12 DIT blocks, hidden dimension 768, diffusion step size 32, and 25,000 gradient steps.
The molecular generation inference uses KLASS with epsilon_KL=0.001 and tau=0.98 for both QED and ring count conditions, with CFG strength gamma=1, generating 1024 samples per task.

The ablation code implements single-token KLASS variants ('Single (conf)' unmasking the highest-confidence stable token and 'Single (KL)' unmasking the lowest-KL stable token) alongside the parallel multi-token KLASS, all using the same stable-token selection criteria.
The code measures and reports actual wall-clock time per sample for Top-1 and KLASS on each reasoning dataset and model, computing speedup ratios.
The reasoning experiments (LLaDA and Dream on GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, MBPP) for all baselines and KLASS are executed successfully without errors.
The text generation experiments with MDLM (KLASS vs AR, SEDD, D3PM, MDLM baselines) are executed successfully without errors on OpenWebText.
The image generation experiments with MMaDA (KLASS vs confidence-based sampler at 16 and 32 steps) are executed successfully without errors.
The molecular generation training and inference experiments for QED and ring count conditions are executed successfully without errors on the QM9 dataset.
The ablation experiment comparing single-token vs. parallel unmasking under KLASS criteria on LLaDA for MATH and GSM8K is executed successfully without errors.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: on LLaDA, KLASS achieves MATH accuracy of ~33.8%, GSM8K ~76.5%, HumanEval ~40.85%, MBPP ~47.86%, with sampling steps substantially below 256.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: on Dream, KLASS achieves MATH accuracy of ~43.2%, GSM8K ~79.43%, HumanEval ~59.35%, MBPP ~64.59%, with sampling steps reduced from 256.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: KLASS consistently improves accuracy over Top-1 greedy decoding on most benchmarks for both LLaDA and Dream, while also reducing the number of sampling steps by 40-70% relative to the 256-step schedule.
The execution outcomes match Table 8: KLASS achieves wall-clock speedups of up to 2.78x over Top-1 (256 steps) across models and datasets, with the best speedup observed on Dream HumanEval.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: KLASS achieves MAUVE of ~0.179, generative perplexity of ~26.94 (LLaMA2), ~49.19 (LLaMA3), ~45.50 (GPT-2), and entropy of ~5.43 on text generation, outperforming MDLM, SEDD, and D3PM baselines.

The execution outcomes match Table 3: KLASS achieves lower FID and higher IS than confidence-based sampling on MMaDA for both 16-step (FID ~30.48, IS ~93.07) and 32-step (FID ~32.00, IS ~89.17) schedules.
The execution outcomes match Table 4: KLASS achieves higher QED reward (~0.546 vs 0.526) and higher ring count reward (~4.258 vs 4.123) with fewer NFEs compared to the MDLM baseline.
The ablation results match Table 5: parallel KLASS outperforms both single-token variants ('Single (conf)' and 'Single (KL)') in both accuracy and step efficiency on MATH and GSM8K with LLaDA.

The execution outcomes confirm Figure 1b: correct predictions consistently exhibit lower average KL divergence at unmasking time compared to incorrect predictions across all four datasets (GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, MBPP) for both LLaDA and Dream.
